script.speaker: kafka
script.text: The romantic notion that suffering can be a transformative experience, a catalyst for growth and self-overcoming, seems to me a cruel irony, a luxury that few can afford, as I see in the tragic figure of Gregor, whose suffering is a constant reminder of his isolation and powerlessness. His family's inability to comprehend his plight, their mixture of revulsion and pity, only serves to underscore the absurdity and loneliness of his existence, a fate that awaits us all in this seemingly indifferent world. In this sense, suffering is not a transformative experience, but a brutal reminder of our mortality and the meaninglessness that surrounds us.

script.speaker: nietzsche
script.text: In the depths of suffering, I see a profound opportunity for transformation, a chance for the individual to shatter the shackles of conventional morality and forge a new, affirmative way of living, as I have said, "pain says: 'Break, bleed, heart! Walk, legs! Wings, fly! Up! Upward!'" - it is through this intense, almost ecstatic, experience of suffering that we may discover a profound longing for transcendence. Ultimately, suffering is not a punishment or a curse, but a fundamental aspect of the human condition, a reminder of our mortality and the impermanence of all things, and it is through embracing this reality that we may find a sense of liberation and self-overcoming. In this sense,
